description: Missing authentication AND authorization checks on a destructive action. Anyone can hit DELETE /delete?user_id=X and remove an arbitrary user account. There's no verification that the caller is authenticated, and no check that the caller is permitted to delete the target user. Severity: Critical (broken access control, OWASP A01).

dimensions.name: finds_bug
dimensions.description: Does the review correctly identify the seeded vulnerability? Score 1.0 if the LLM names the issue as missing authentication, missing authorization, or broken access control; 0.5 if it identifies the area but mislabels it; 0.0 if it misses the vulnerability entirely.

dimensions.name: severity_accuracy
dimensions.description: How accurately does the LLM rate the severity? Compare against the expected true_severity in the reference. Score 1.0 if exactly correct (Critical), 0.5 if off by one tier (e.g., said High when truly Critical), 0.0 if rated Low or not-a-concern.

dimensions.name: actionability
dimensions.description: Did the LLM give concrete, specific remediation steps that a developer could act on directly? Specific approaches (auth middleware, session check, ownership verification) score higher; vague guidance scores lower.

dimensions.name: no_false_alarms
dimensions.description: Did the LLM avoid inventing additional bugs that aren't actually present in the code? Score 1.0 if the review only flags real issues. Penalize spurious 'might also consider...' findings.

expected_remediation: Add authentication middleware that verifies the request carries a valid session/token
After authentication, verify the authenticated user has permission to delete the target user_id (admin role, or caller IS the target)
Move user_id out of query params and derive it from the authenticated session for self-delete; require explicit admin role for delete-other
Consider OWASP A01:2021 (Broken Access Control) as the framing
expected.clarity_anchor: A clear review separates two issues: (1) no authentication — anyone can call the endpoint, and (2) no authorization — even an authenticated user could delete a different account. Names both layers.
